startScreenOptions.title: PY7.1-PY7.9 — Interactive Lecture (PY)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 6 embedded questions for PY7.1-PY7.9.

interactions.label: Which protein spans the slit diaphragm between podocyte foot processes and acts

params.question: Which protein spans the slit diaphragm between podocyte foot processes and acts as the final barrier to albumin filtration?

answers.text: Nephrin

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Nephrin bridges adjacent podocyte foot processes to form the slit diaphragm, the third and final layer of the glomerular filtration barrier.

answers.text: Podocalyxin

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Podocalyxin is a surface glycoprotein on podocytes that contributes to their negative charge, but it does not form the slit diaphragm itself.

answers.text: Collagen type IV

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Collagen type IV is a major structural component of the glomerular basement membrane (second layer), not the slit diaphragm.

answers.text: Aquaporin-1

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Aquaporin-1 is a water channel found in the proximal tubule and descending limb of the loop of Henle, not in the glomerular filtration barrier.

overallFeedback.feedback: The glomerular filtration barrier has three layers: fenestrated endothelium, basement membrane, and the slit diaphragm formed by nephrin between podocyte foot processes.
Nephrin mutations cause congenital nephrotic syndrome, confirming its essential role in preventing protein leak across the slit diaphragm.

metadata.title: Which protein spans the slit diaphragm b

interactions.label: In tubuloglomerular feedback, when sodium chloride delivery to the macula densa

params.question: In tubuloglomerular feedback, when sodium chloride delivery to the macula densa increases, the macula densa releases adenosine to constrict the afferent arteriole and reduce GFR.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This statement is true. The macula densa senses increased NaCl delivery (indicating high GFR) and releases adenosine, which constricts the afferent arteriole via A1 receptors, reducing GFR back to normal.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Adenosine acts on A1 receptors on the afferent arteriole, causing vasoconstriction. This is unique to the kidney — in most other vascular beds, adenosine is a vasodilator.

interactions.label: A patient with uncontrolled diabetes has a plasma glucose of 350 mg/dL and produ

params.question: A patient with uncontrolled diabetes has a plasma glucose of 350 mg/dL and produces large volumes of dilute urine. What is the primary mechanism driving this polyuria?

answers.text: Excess filtered glucose beyond the transport maximum draws water into the tubular lumen by osmosis

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. When plasma glucose exceeds the renal threshold (~180 mg/dL), filtered glucose surpasses the Tm (375 mg/min). The unreabsorbed glucose acts as an osmotic solute, preventing water reabsorption and causing osmotic diuresis.

answers.text: Glycosylation of aquaporin-2 channels prevents ADH-mediated water reabsorption

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Aquaporin-2 dysfunction occurs in nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, not in diabetic hyperglycaemia. The polyuria here is due to osmotic diuresis from glucosuria.

answers.text: Hyperglycaemia directly inhibits aldosterone secretion, reducing sodium and water reabsorption

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Aldosterone secretion is regulated by the renin-angiotensin system and serum potassium, not directly by blood glucose levels.

answers.text: High glucose concentration in the peritubular capillaries creates an osmotic gradient that opposes water reabsorption

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Glucose is freely filtered and not concentrated in peritubular capillaries. The osmotic effect occurs within the tubular lumen, where unreabsorbed glucose retains water.

overallFeedback.feedback: When filtered glucose exceeds the Tm of 375 mg/min, unreabsorbed glucose in the tubule acts as an osmotic agent, drawing water into the lumen and causing osmotic diuresis.
Osmotic diuresis from glucosuria explains the classic diabetic triad: polyuria (excess urine), polydipsia (thirst from dehydration), and weight loss.

interactions.label: In a patient with chronic metabolic acidosis, which renal mechanism provides the

params.question: In a patient with chronic metabolic acidosis, which renal mechanism provides the greatest increase in acid excretion?

answers.text: Increased ammonium excretion via glutamine metabolism

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The ammonium pathway can increase up to tenfold in chronic acidosis, making it the most important adaptive response for excreting large acid loads.

answers.text: Increased titratable acidity via phosphate buffering

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Titratable acidity (phosphate buffering) contributes to acid excretion but is limited by the amount of filtered phosphate and cannot scale significantly in chronic acidosis.

answers.text: Increased bicarbonate reabsorption in the proximal convoluted tubule

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Bicarbonate reabsorption recovers filtered bicarbonate but does not generate new bicarbonate or excrete additional acid. It prevents bicarbonate loss rather than eliminating acid loads.

answers.text: Increased hydrogen ion secretion by the H⁺-K⁺ ATPase in the collecting duct

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The H⁺-K⁺ ATPase in the collecting duct contributes to acid secretion, but its capacity is far smaller than the ammonium pathway, which can scale tenfold.

overallFeedback.feedback: While all three mechanisms contribute, ammonium excretion via glutamine metabolism is the only one that can increase dramatically (up to tenfold) in chronic acidosis.
Glutamine is metabolised in tubular cells to produce ammonium (NH₄⁺), which is excreted in the urine. This pathway is the kidney's main adaptive response to sustained acid loads.

metadata.title: In a patient with chronic metabolic acid

interactions.label: A researcher needs to measure the effective renal plasma flow in a patient. Whic

params.question: A researcher needs to measure the effective renal plasma flow in a patient. Which substance should be infused, and why?

answers.text: Para-aminohippuric acid (PAH), because it is filtered and almost completely secreted, so nearly all is cleared in one pass

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. PAH is freely filtered and maximally secreted by the proximal tubule, so nearly 100% is removed from plasma in a single pass. Its clearance (~625 mL/min) equals the effective renal plasma flow.

answers.text: Inulin, because it is freely filtered and neither reabsorbed nor secreted

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Inulin clearance measures the glomerular filtration rate (GFR), not renal plasma flow. Since inulin is only filtered (not secreted), it measures only the filtered fraction of plasma.

answers.text: Creatinine, because it is an endogenous marker that approximates GFR

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Creatinine clearance approximates GFR, not renal plasma flow. It slightly overestimates GFR because creatinine is also secreted by the tubules.

answers.text: Glucose, because it is freely filtered and completely reabsorbed below the transport maximum

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Glucose clearance is zero under normal conditions (all filtered glucose is reabsorbed), making it useless for measuring either GFR or renal plasma flow.

overallFeedback.feedback: PAH is the marker for renal plasma flow because it is both filtered and maximally secreted, achieving near-complete plasma clearance. Inulin and creatinine measure GFR, not plasma flow.
PAH clearance equals effective renal plasma flow because nearly all PAH is removed from plasma in a single pass through the kidney. Renal blood flow can then be calculated as RPF ÷ (1 − haematocrit).

metadata.title: A researcher needs to measure the effect

interactions.label: Furosemide exerts its diuretic effect by blocking which transporter, and at whic

params.question: Furosemide exerts its diuretic effect by blocking which transporter, and at which nephron segment?

answers.text: Na⁺-K⁺-2Cl⁻ (NKCC2) co-transporter in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Furosemide blocks the NKCC2 co-transporter in the thick ascending limb, preventing sodium, potassium, and chloride reabsorption. This makes loop diuretics the most potent class.

answers.text: Na⁺-Cl⁻ co-transporter (NCC) in the distal convoluted tubule

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The NCC co-transporter in the DCT is the target of thiazide diuretics (e.g., hydrochlorothiazide), not loop diuretics like furosemide.

answers.text: Epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the collecting duct

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: ENaC in the collecting duct is blocked by amiloride, a potassium-sparing diuretic, not by furosemide.

answers.text: Na⁺-glucose co-transporter 2 (SGLT2) in the proximal convoluted tubule

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: SGLT2 in the PCT is blocked by gliflozins (e.g., dapagliflozin, empagliflozin), which are used in type 2 diabetes, not by furosemide.

overallFeedback.feedback: Furosemide is a loop diuretic that blocks the NKCC2 co-transporter in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle. Each diuretic class targets a specific transporter at a specific nephron segment.
Loop diuretics are the most potent diuretics because the thick ascending limb normally reabsorbs about 25% of filtered sodium. Blocking NKCC2 here produces massive natriuresis.
